---
title: "OOM"
subtitle: "Pack tensors into HBM before you crash."
description: "A browser mini-game from MLSys Playground. Tensor-Tetris in your GPU's high-bandwidth memory. Stack activations, gradients, optimizer states, and KV cache without overflowing."
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="/assets/games/common.css">
          <script src="/assets/games/common.js" defer></script>
          <script src="/assets/games/oom.js" defer></script>
---

```{=html}
<div class="mlsp-game-container" role="region" aria-label="OOM mini-game">
  <canvas id="mlsp-canvas" class="mlsp-game-canvas" width="680" height="460"></canvas>
  <div class="mlsp-game-hud">
    <span class="mlsp-score">score <span id="mlsp-score">0</span></span>
    <span>← → move · ↓ down · space drop · <kbd>R</kbd> retry</span>
  </div>
</div>

<div id="mlsp-aha-slot"></div>

<script>
(function mountOom(){
  function boot() {
    if (!window.MLSP || !MLSP.games || !MLSP.games.oom) return setTimeout(boot, 30);
    var canvas = document.getElementById("mlsp-canvas");
    var $score = document.getElementById("mlsp-score");
    var ahaSlot = document.getElementById("mlsp-aha-slot");
    var api = MLSP.games.oom(canvas, {
      onScoreChange: function(s) { $score.textContent = s.score; },
      onGameOver: function() {
        MLSP.showAhaCard(ahaSlot, api.ahaLabel, api.ahaText);
      },
      onRetry: function() { window.location.reload(); }
    });
  }
  boot();
})();
</script>
```

## How it works

Tetris, but with tensor blocks. Each falling block represents a real GPU memory citizen:

- **Activations** (blue, wide) — outputs of forward-pass layers, kept around for backward
- **Gradients** (red, narrow) — what backward computes
- **Optimizer states** (orange, square) — Adam keeps moments per parameter, ~2× model size
- **KV cache** (green, tall) — attention's growing memory hog during inference

Pack them into the HBM box without the stack reaching the top. Complete a full row and that memory frees up — same way real allocators reclaim contiguous space.

## Why this matters

ML practitioners spend a lot of time tuning batch size, gradient accumulation, and recomputation strategies because GPU memory is *the* binding constraint at scale. Run out and your training crashes. The shape of the constraint — different tensor types, different lifetimes, fragmentation under live allocation — is what real frameworks like PyTorch's allocator manage every step.

---

*Part of [MLSys Playground](/games/).*
